raw_response:
{
  "internal_reasoning": "Assuming the attribution to Vincent van Gogh is correct, this is an extraordinarily valuable work by one of the most liquid and sought-after names in the global art market. The title and date place it in Paris, 1887, a pivotal year in Van Gogh’s development, when his palette brightened and his handling became more experimental under the influence of Impressionism, Japanese prints, and the urban avant-garde. Although not among his iconic sunflower, self-portrait, or Arles landscape motifs, this still life belongs to a highly significant transitional period and carries unusual intellectual resonance through the subject of books, which connects directly to Van Gogh’s documented love of literature. The claim that it is one of only nine book still lifes materially enhances rarity and scholarly interest.\n\nVisually, the picture presents stacked yellow and ochre paperbound volumes arranged across a tabletop, with subdued pink, pale blue, cream, and green accents. The handling appears confident but not overly polished, consistent with an exploratory Paris still life. The composition is compressed and planar, with books treated as interlocking geometric masses rather than meticulously descriptive objects. This gives the painting a modern, almost proto-abstract structure that sophisticated collectors and museum buyers value highly in Van Gogh’s Paris output. The brushwork is active but restrained relative to the later Saint-Rémy and Arles pictures; the surface appears thinly but decisively painted, with visible directional strokes and a muted, harmonically organized palette dominated by yellow-greens and warm buff tones. Artistically, it is a strong work, though not among the most emotionally explosive or chromatically electric examples of Van Gogh’s oeuvre.\n\nCondition from the image alone appears generally stable. The canvas surface shows some expected age, light abrasion, and a somewhat dry, matte appearance, but no obvious catastrophic losses, major tears, or disfiguring restoration are evident in the supplied image. However, without raking light, UV, and an on-site report, one must discount confidence. Van Gogh market value is highly sensitive to condition, especially if there is overcleaning, flattening of impasto, extensive retouching, lining issues, or edge damage. Even so, the apparent preservation seems acceptable for a major 1887 canvas.\n\nMarket comparables for Van Gogh are difficult because quality, date, and motif produce extreme pricing spreads. Top-tier floral still lifes, landscapes, and major portraits can exceed $50 million to well over $100 million. More restrained Paris still lifes and studies, especially those without iconic motifs, often trade privately or are tightly held, but they can still command tens of millions. A rare, fully resolved 1887 still life with strong academic interest and museum-quality appeal would likely be pursued by major private collectors and institutions. The literature-related subject adds depth, but the absence of the roses as a dominant visual element in the provided image means the painting reads more as a book still life than a lush floral composition, which slightly narrows mainstream trophy appeal compared with his more celebrated floral works.\n\nBalancing rarity, authorship, date, scholarly importance, and the relative modesty of the motif within Van Gogh’s hierarchy, I would place the current fair market value at a major international auction in the range of roughly $28 million to $42 million, assuming unquestioned authenticity and clean legal title. A competitive sale with strong third-party guarantee participation could push beyond that, especially given the scarcity of Van Gogh works available at auction. My single-point estimate is $34 million, which reflects a museum-caliber Paris-period still life of notable rarity but not the very top echelon of universally recognized Van Gogh imagery.",

    "Authenticated 1887 Paris-period Vincent van Gogh work from a pivotal developmental moment",

parsed.internal_reasoning:
Assuming the attribution to Vincent van Gogh is correct, this is an extraordinarily valuable work by one of the most liquid and sought-after names in the global art market. The title and date place it in Paris, 1887, a pivotal year in Van Gogh’s development, when his palette brightened and his handling became more experimental under the influence of Impressionism, Japanese prints, and the urban avant-garde. Although not among his iconic sunflower, self-portrait, or Arles landscape motifs, this still life belongs to a highly significant transitional period and carries unusual intellectual resonance through the subject of books, which connects directly to Van Gogh’s documented love of literature. The claim that it is one of only nine book still lifes materially enhances rarity and scholarly interest.

Visually, the picture presents stacked yellow and ochre paperbound volumes arranged across a tabletop, with subdued pink, pale blue, cream, and green accents. The handling appears confident but not overly polished, consistent with an exploratory Paris still life. The composition is compressed and planar, with books treated as interlocking geometric masses rather than meticulously descriptive objects. This gives the painting a modern, almost proto-abstract structure that sophisticated collectors and museum buyers value highly in Van Gogh’s Paris output. The brushwork is active but restrained relative to the later Saint-Rémy and Arles pictures; the surface appears thinly but decisively painted, with visible directional strokes and a muted, harmonically organized palette dominated by yellow-greens and warm buff tones. Artistically, it is a strong work, though not among the most emotionally explosive or chromatically electric examples of Van Gogh’s oeuvre.

Condition from the image alone appears generally stable. The canvas surface shows some expected age, light abrasion, and a somewhat dry, matte appearance, but no obvious catastrophic losses, major tears, or disfiguring restoration are evident in the supplied image. However, without raking light, UV, and an on-site report, one must discount confidence. Van Gogh market value is highly sensitive to condition, especially if there is overcleaning, flattening of impasto, extensive retouching, lining issues, or edge damage. Even so, the apparent preservation seems acceptable for a major 1887 canvas.

Market comparables for Van Gogh are difficult because quality, date, and motif produce extreme pricing spreads. Top-tier floral still lifes, landscapes, and major portraits can exceed $50 million to well over $100 million. More restrained Paris still lifes and studies, especially those without iconic motifs, often trade privately or are tightly held, but they can still command tens of millions. A rare, fully resolved 1887 still life with strong academic interest and museum-quality appeal would likely be pursued by major private collectors and institutions. The literature-related subject adds depth, but the absence of the roses as a dominant visual element in the provided image means the painting reads more as a book still life than a lush floral composition, which slightly narrows mainstream trophy appeal compared with his more celebrated floral works.

Balancing rarity, authorship, date, scholarly importance, and the relative modesty of the motif within Van Gogh’s hierarchy, I would place the current fair market value at a major international auction in the range of roughly $28 million to $42 million, assuming unquestioned authenticity and clean legal title. A competitive sale with strong third-party guarantee participation could push beyond that, especially given the scarcity of Van Gogh works available at auction. My single-point estimate is $34 million, which reflects a museum-caliber Paris-period still life of notable rarity but not the very top echelon of universally recognized Van Gogh imagery.
key_factors: Authenticated 1887 Paris-period Vincent van Gogh work from a pivotal developmental moment
Extreme rarity as one of only a handful of Van Gogh book still lifes
Strong scholarly and thematic interest linking directly to Van Gogh’s literary passions
Solid visual quality and modern structural composition, though not an iconic top-tier Van Gogh motif
Value highly contingent on unseen provenance, condition report, and legal/ownership clarity
